summary: Fournier reflects on her unexpected transition from team lead to head of engineering after her boss's departure. Despite her initial confidence that she could handle the role, she quickly discovered that leading 20 people and owning organizational outcomes is fundamentally different from managing a small team. She learned that her words and moods now ripple throughout the entire organization, and that recruiting requires a completely different level of ownership. Her mother's simple advice to 'smile more' became her key strategy for projecting calm and enabling her team to thrive.
key_points: The jump from managing 6 people to leading 20 involves exponentially more responsibility, not just incrementally more
Without a buffer above you, every gap in your leadership is felt directly by your team
As the person in charge, your stress, impatience, and snide comments echo and amplify throughout the organization
Owning recruiting end-to-end is completely different from just helping with interviews
Projecting positivity through something as simple as smiling can transform team dynamics and enable people to step up
money_quotes: Call it the Dunning-Kruger effect, or simply call it arrogance, but I think if you had asked me before I was put into this position whether I could do the job well, I would have told you certainly yes.
When there is no buffer in your department above you that people can go to when your guidance is lacking, the weight of that responsibility is 10 times what you ever expected.
A sudden transition of leadership even in a solid organization such as ours stirs up long-simmering conflicts.
When I speak harshly to people over technical matters, it is yelling even if I don't intend it to be.
One snide comment about a decision or a design invites others to sneer at that decision along with me.
When I smile, I see people step up and they take on bigger responsibilities than they've ever had, and knock them out of the park over and over again.
Because change is scary and hard, but in the long run, it's good.
key_insight: The transition to senior leadership reveals that your emotional state and casual comments now echo throughout the entire organization, making self-awareness and intentional positivity essential leadership skills.
